title: How Large Are Bequest Motives? Estimates Based on Health Shocks
abstract: I analyze the inter vivo transfers and bequest decisions of 700,000 individuals during a period when the decision maker receives negative news regarding their life expectancy. The event that initiates the news is a health outcome. Expected mortality increases both the likelihood of transferring wealth to the next generation and the amount transferred. The size of the inter vivo transfer and bequest are positively related to the wealth of the parent and the severity of the diagnosis, regardless of diagnosis-specific demand for informal care. Using a structural life cycle model, I estimate the bequest parameters that are consistent with the causal effect estimates.

article.1. Data and Institutional Setting:
JEL D1 - Household Behavior and Family Economics D14 - Household Saving; Personal Finance D31 - Personal Income, Wealth, and Their Distributions E21 - Consumption; Saving; Wealth I12 - Health Behavior Issue Section: Article Editor: Tarun Ramadorai Tarun Ramadorai Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Intergenerational relationships and bequest motives play a crucial role in economics and finance. Classic examples include saving and consumption decisions, capital accumulation, and economic growth (Kotlikoff and Summers 1981;Modigliani 1988;Barro 1974) and education of children (Becker and Tomes 1979). Examples from finance include access to capital among young entrepreneursBlanchflower and Oswald 1998;Ellul, Pagano, and Panunzi 2010and demand for insurance and financial products (Koijen, Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo 2016;Nakajima and Telyukova 2017).

Despite the pivotal role of the bequest motive in economic analysis, its empirical relevance remains an open question. The main challenge is to separate bequest motives from precautionary saving motives. This is because these saving motives can generate indistinguishable saving profiles over the life cycle (Dynan, Skinner, and Zeldes 2002).

This paper offers a solution to the identification problem. The idea is that sudden changes in life expectancy trigger a change in the elderly individual’s saving plan, which is informative about saving motives. If individuals save mainly to hedge against personal lifespan uncertainty, personal wealth will be responsive to changes in expected mortality, but heirs’ wealth will be unaffected. In contrast, if individuals have bequest motives, the total wealth of the parent and the children will be less responsive to changes in expected mortality of the parent and heirs’ wealth is likely to increase.

I use the severity of a health crisis with respect to mortality as an instrument for a decrease in life expectancy. To understand the reaction of wealth to mortality, I combine administrative data on health, financial, and demographic characteristics of approximately 700,000 individuals living in Norway and the Netherlands. Causality comes from exploiting the fact that the exact timing of a diagnosis is as good as random, calculable to within a few years. The identifying assumption has support in epidemiological research and is often referred to as the “prevention paradox,” described by Geoffrey Rose.1

The paper begins by formalizing the link between the revision of survival expectations and saving choices under two different models of saving behavior. The unrestricted model is a standard life cycle model expanded to include inter vivo transfers as inHurd, Smith, and Zissimopoulos (2011). Under fairly general assumptions, the unrestricted model predicts that a higher mortality risk will cause an increase in inter vivo transfers. The restricted model is identical to the unrestricted model, but without transfers.

My four main contributions are as follows. Using the intergenerational links in the Norwegian data set, I first show that the wealth of the children in the treatment group increased by about 10%after one of the parents has been diagnosed with cancer. In contrast, the sum of the children’s and the parent’s wealth appears almost unaffected. These results suggest that the drop in parents’ wealth is offset by an increase in the children’s wealth. While these findings may reflect both accidental and declared bequests, the complementary analysis for the Netherlands, which is based on the declared transfer only, provides a similar conclusion.

Next, I test whether wealth influences how mortality risk affects transfers and bequests. If bequest motives are nonhomothetic, then the probability of making a transfer will be zero for a subset of the population.2Under the alternative of homothetic preferences, the probability of making a transfer is independent of wealth. My estimates suggest nonhomothetic bequest preferences: a health crisis among individuals below the median in the wealth distribution increases the likelihood of transferring within the next couple of years by 0.24%⁠. Compared to this group, individuals between the median and the upper quartile of the wealth distribution are about three times more likely to transfer, and those in the upper quartile are about eight times more likely to transfer.

The cross-sectional variation in expected mortality makes it possible to test whether higher mortality risk diagnoses lead to larger transfers than low mortality risk diagnoses. I find that more severe diagnoses cause larger increases in children’s wealth. Complementary analysis based on Dutch data reveals that individuals diagnosed with a high-mortality risk diagnosis are roughly three to four times more likely to provide inter vivo transfers than individuals diagnosed with a low-mortality risk diagnosis. Notably, the high- and low-mortality risk groups are constructed such that the demand for informal care is the same in the two groups. Therefore, the likelihood of inter vivo transfer increases with mortality risk, not the need for informal care.

My final contribution is to estimate the parameters that determine the utility from bequest in a standard “warm-glow” specification, in which the parent’s utility is derived from the bequeathed amount. These estimates are based on indirect inference. The identification comes from comparing a vector of selected difference-in-differences estimates and summary statistics from the data to a vector of similar statistics produced by the life cycle model extended to include inter vivo transfers.3The estimated bequest parameters reveal a strong bequest motive for the wealthier part of the population.

The results of structural estimation are reminiscent of those byKoijen, Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016), who find, using survey data, that the distribution of bequest motives mimics that of the wealth distribution. Collectively, these parameters provide guidance on how to calibrate a bequest function in life cycle models. More broadly, these parameters suggest major inequality in inheritance; with financial gifts over the life cycle as an important contributor to cross-sectional variation in wealth (see, e.g.,Charles and Hurst 2003;Fagereng, Mogstad, and Roenning 2021;Boserup, Kopczuk, and Kreiner 2016).

A large body of literature has studied saving motives. The early empirical literature aimed to separate bequest motives from precautionary savings against uncertainty about life expectancy. Using survey data,Gale and Scholz (1994)andLaitner and Juster (1996)estimate that about 20%of lifetime private net worth comes from intentional estate. A novel contribution of this study is that my estimates reflect what people do, and not what they say they have done in the past, or are planning to do in the future. The inference is therefore not subject to the critique byKopczuk and Lupton (2007)regarding whether individuals can accurately distinguish their inherited wealth from wealth stemming from their labor income or concerns about systematic understatements of inter vivo transfers (see, e.g.,Kurz 1984).

Hurd (1987,1989)proposes an alternative identification strategy by estimating the difference between the change in wealth for households with and without children. He shows that households with children decumulate wealth faster than those without children; a fact that I replicate in my data. Hurd’s finding is often used as evidence against bequest motives. However, as I show, at least part of the asset deaccumulation of households with children reflects inter vivo transfers, which are direct evidence of bequest motives, not against it.

The more recent literature, following the seminal contribution ofGourinchas and Parker (2002), uses structural estimation together with multiple choices made by the elderly to identify saving motives. In an important paper,Ameriks et al. (2011)use responses to survey questions about peoples’ state-contingent plans. They find evidence of significant heterogeneity in the bequest motives. They are minimal for at least a substantial minority of the population and are on average higher for those with children than for those without children.Lockwood (2018)shows that models without bequests cannot simultaneously match the low rates of long-term care insurance coverage among middle-class retirees and the slow drawdown of wealth among middle-class retirees.Inkmann and Michaelides (2012)conclude that the life insurance holdings of U.K. individuals are consistent with bequest motives.De Nardi, French, and Jones (2016a)match Medicaid recipiency rates and transfer amounts. To match assets in this environment, the model attributes part of the savings to bequest motives.

My paper adds to the growing body of work on international comparative household finance. While comparative studies on household behavior can offer multiple advantages relative to studies on a single market (for a discussion, seeBadarinza, Campbell, and Ramadorai 2016;Badarinza, Balasubramaniam, and Ramadorai 2019), its main advantage in this study is external validity. First, in both countries, the direct and indirect costs associated with unfavorable health outcomes are small. Second, inter vivo giving is likely to be important because inheritance taxation incentivizes inter vivo transfers. Third, the government covers most long-term care costs, which makes strategic use of financial gifts and bequest to elicit services from the children unlikely.4In sum, these features make it possible to sidestep several common threats to the identification of bequest motives. In addition, a cross-country comparison of the same mechanism inferred from different shocks (i.e., diagnoses), time period, and cultures work as a first pass of the generalizability of the findings.
I begin by describing my data sources and the advantages of the Norwegian and the Dutch data. I emphasize the aspects of the institutional setting that are the most relevant for the identification strategy. TheInternet Appendixincludes additional information.

1.1 The data set

The data set is the result of merging register data collected by Tax Administrations with detailed medical records for two countries: Norway and the Netherlands. The Norwegian data set includes detailed data on cancer, inter alia, information on cancer type, stage, and publicly available 5-year relative survival expectations. These data come from the Cancer Registry of Norway (CRN). The CRN has registered all cancer cases in Norway since 1953. Mandatory reporting by clinicians and pathologists and death certificates ensure high-quality data on the date of diagnosis, the patient’s age at diagnosis, gender, tumor location (International Classification of Diseases, 10th revision (ICD-10)), and the stage upon diagnosis (local, regional, distant, or unknown). Together, the 23 main cancer types and the four cancer stages provide cross-sectional variation in expected mortality. The Norwegian Tax Administration and Statistics Norway provide the financial and demographic data, including links between parents and children. Except for the cancer data and the generational links, the data set is similar in content to that used byFagereng, Gottlieb, and Guiso (2017).

The Dutch data set is provided by Statistics Netherlands (CBS) and covers the Dutch population. It includes administrative data on medical diagnoses, household wealth, demographics, and declared inter vivo transfers. In addition, it includes a nationwide survey on informal care.

The two data sets complement each other. The Norwegian data set contains detailed data on cancer, including publicly available 5-year relative survival expectations. The Dutch data set includes a complete set of diseases, essential for extrapolating the estimated treatment effects outside cancer. Data on declared transfers make it possible to analyze inter vivo transfers along the extensive and intensive margin. Relying on declared transfers also helps to narrow down the possible motivations for making a bequest to intentional ones. Finally, the survey data make it possible to control for informal care, which might confound the estimated effect of mortality on saving choices.

1.2 The institutional setting

I focus on one-person households (henceforth singles) in Norway and the Netherlands, aged above 59 years mainly relying on pension income.5The unit of analysis and the institutional setting make it possible to avoid several common threats to the identification of bequest motives.

Starting with the unit of analysis, the response of family households (henceforth couples) to the same crisis is potentially influenced by the equivalence scale in the consumption and bequest motive toward the spouse (see, e.g.,Bernheim et al. 2003;Inkmann and Michaelides 2012;De Nardi, French, and Jones 2016b). Depending on these parameters, changes in expected mortality may decrease or increase savings for more reasons than those we can identify, which would threaten the identification.

Regarding the institutional setting, first, in both countries, pension income is effectively an annuity income stream that is unrelated to health. This feature, combined with generous disability benefits, mitigates any indirect wealth effect through loss of human capital after negative health crises (see, e.g.,Smith 2004). Second, mandatory health insurance covers all medical expenditures above a minimum threshold, which prevents changes in expected mortality and deterioration of health to mechanically increase health spending, or precautionary saving.6The final point is related to long-term care (LTC). Norway has a tax-based, universal public LTC scheme that reduces the need for children to engage in informal care. The Dutch LTC system is similarly generous and comprehensive.7

The Norwegian and Dutch tax systems both favor inter vivo transfers due to tax exemptions and progressive tax rates. In Norway, while living, each parent can transfer a tax-exempt amount of about 5,000 to 6,000 EUR per child per year. These transfers do not affect the tax-free part of the inheritance, which I will explain below, but unused transfers cannot be used later. There is no inheritance tax below 60,000 EUR, after which it increases to between 6%and 15%⁠. The exact tax rate depends on the transfer amount and the recipient; children pay less tax than grandchildren. In the Netherlands, the tax system is similar. Each year, parents (together) can give around 5,000 to 6,000 EUR per child tax exempt. As in Norway, inheritance tax rates are progressive and depend on the recipient; again, children have the highest tax exemption (around 20,000 EUR) and pay the lowest tax rates, which are between 10%and 20%⁠. Furthermore, once in a lifetime, parents can transfer to each of their children a tax-exempt amount of between approximately 25,000 to 50,000 EUR depending on how the recipient uses the gift.8
article.2. Research Design:
I formalize the link between the revision in survival expectations and saving choices under two different models of saving behavior, both subject to risks and constraints consistent with the institutional setting of the paper. The model fulfills two purposes. First, it shows that the existence of bequest motives can be inferred from the sensitivity of inter vivo transfers to mortality risk. Second, it provides the building blocks for quantification of the bequest motive in Section5. Because the model serves as a starting point for the structural estimation, it is slightly more general than what is necessary to fulfill its first purpose.

2.1 A model of saving behavior and transfers

I consider a life cycle model with inter vivo transfers as inHurd, Smith, and Zissimopoulos (2011). The unit of analysis is a one-person household in retirement indexed byi⁠. The model consists of a series of 1-year periods, indexedt⁠, starting at retirement, and ending at the year of death. The retiree has liquid wealthWi,t≥0⁠, and potentially illiquid wealth,REi≥0⁠, pension incomeYi⁠, and partial insurance. Borrowing constraint ensures that the liquid wealth is nonnegative. The illiquid wealth and pension income are given in the first year of retirement and remain constant. In practice, this means that pension income and illiquid assets, such as real estate wealth, remain at current levels in real terms, and that the retiree does not sell illiquid assets.9The insurance is partial because it does not cover all possible expenditures,Zi,t+1⁠. Social security ensures that expenditure shocks are always less than pension income. The probability of dying in the subsequent year,mi,t=m(hi,t,t)⁠, depends on aget⁠, and current health,hi,t⁠, which is exogenous and only affects the risk of mortality.10Given these resources and mortality expectations,mi,t⁠, the retiree chooses consumptionCi,t>0⁠, and possibly inter vivo transfersGi,t≥0to maximize expected lifetime utility. In case of death, any remaining wealth is left as inheritance.

LetτG(⋅)andτB(⋅)be functions that map gross gifts into net of tax amounts. Omitting the subscripti⁠, the utility from consumption,Ct⁠, and transfers,Gt⁠, then can be represented by the additive utility functionU(Ct,τG(Gt))=u(Ct)+ν(τG(Gt))⁠. WithRE+Wt+1representing the net worth of a deceased individual, the realized utility of the bequest isν(τB(RE+Wt+1))⁠. I make two assumptions about preferences. First, the marginal utility of personal consumption is strictly monotonic, and all individuals prefer a stable consumption path. This assumption implies a positive first derivative and a negative second derivative of the consumption function,u(⋅)⁠. Second, the utility function for inter vivo transfers and bequest,ν(⋅)⁠, can be either linear or concave. In either case, the utility from inter vivo transfers (⁠ν(τG(⋅)⁠) is an increasing and concave function. The reason is that parents can give an annual tax-free amount while they are alive, which gives diminishing marginal utility benefits from transfers in any given period.

To establish a testable hypothesis for the existence of bequest motives analytically, I make two additional technical assumptions, and a simplifying one.11First, I assume that the utilities from inter vivo transfers and bequest are twice-differentiable smooth functions. Second, I assume mortality risk is a smooth function of health and age. Third, I assume that inter vivo and bequest are taxed equally (i.e.,τG(X)=τB(X)⁠). To ease the exposition, but without loss of generality, I work with partial derivatives of consumption and transfers with respect to mortality risk directly, rather than indirectly through health.

2.2 Identification of bequest motives

I now use the model to illustrate the identification problem associated with inferring bequest motives from wealth data, and then to present an alternative. The envelope condition provides insight. It implies that individuals will equate the marginal utility of current consumption and transfers and the expected discounted marginal utility of wealth. I denote the policy functions for consumption and transfers that solve Equation ( 1 ) by c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) and g ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) ⁠ . The first-order conditions for an individual with a bequest motive are u ′ ( c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) ) − m t E t [ ν ′ ( τ B ( W t + 1 + R E ) ) ] − ( 1 − m t ) E t [ V W ( S t + 1 , t + 1 ) ] = 0 , (2) ν ′ ( τ G ( g ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) ) ) − m t E t [ ν ′ ( τ B ( W t + 1 + R E ) ) ] − ( 1 − m t ) E t [ V W ( S t + 1 , t + 1 ) ] = 0. (3)

Let V W > 0 denote the partial derivative of the value function with respect to liquid wealth. By the inverse function theorem consumption equals: c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) = u ′ − 1 ( m t E t [ ν ′ ( W t + 1 + R E ) ] + ( 1 − m t ) E t [ V W ( S t + 1 , t + 1 ) ] ) . (4)

The consumption function in Equation (4) illustrates the problem of separating bequest motives from precautionary saving motives using wealth data, as highlighted inDynan, Skinner, and Zeldes (2002). The reason is that spending can be low for many reasons other than bequest motives.12Longer than average life expectancy and strong precautionary saving motives, which drive up the marginal value of wealth, are two alternative explanations to bequest for the slow wealth decumulation. The alternative identification strategy, which I use, does not suffer from this identification problem.

Proposition 1 (A test for bequest motive) . If individuals have bequest motives and inheritance taxation is progressive, then higher mortality risk will increase transfers.

Proof See Appendix A . ■

Proposition 1 remains valid under the alternative specification of health-dependent utility. The reason is simple. There are no reasons to transfer wealth to children in the absence of bequest motives.

2.3 Reduced-form empirical strategies

While we cannot randomly manipulate individuals’ life expectancy to identify bequest motives, this paper comes close to such a setting. The idea is to exploit that, conditional on being diagnosed with a disease that increases mortality risk over the next few years, the exact timing is as good as random. There is general empirical support for the identifying assumption in the medical literature; even making predictions from statistical models about whether a specific individual will be diagnosed with a particular disease is difficult for most medical risks. Some individuals in the low-risk, or unexposed category, will develop the disease, whereas most of those exposed will remain healthy (Rockhill, Kawachi, and Colditz 2000). The lack of predictive power of well-known risk factors is often referred to as the prevention paradox, which Geoffrey Rose first described in the early 1980s. Clearly, making predictions about the exact date of diagnosis is even more difficult.

To make the research design explicit, I postulate that the timing of a cancer diagnosis is unpredictable within 3 to 4 years, conditional on age and time-invariant individual characteristics. The research design accounts nonparametrically for latent variables that may correlate with both the treatment and the outcome. Consequently, if cancer is preceded by gradual health deterioration that correlates with saving behavior, such effects would be averaged out. Individual fixed effects remove any potential links between the timing of diagnosis and personal characteristics, such as if more risk-averse individuals receive earlier cancer scans than individuals who are less risk averse.13

The empirical specifications for Norway and the Netherlands are slightly different because of the large difference in the sample size and heterogeneity in diseases. In the case of Norway, which only includes cancer diagnoses, I use a dynamic specification to validate the research design, and to formally test the identifying assumption, and a restricted version to estimate causal effects. For each individual i living in Norway at calendar time t ⁠ , I denote the years since the diagnosis year E i by K i , t = t − E i ⁠ . I include k ∈ { − 3 , − 2 } leads of treatment and k ∈ { 0 , 1 , 2 } effect estimates. Excluding k ∈ { − 4 , − 1 } is the minimum number of restrictions for point identification, as discussed formally in Borusyak and Jaravel (2017) . The resultant event-study regression is ln ⁡ W i , t = α i + β i , t + ∑ k = − 4 3 γ k { K i , t = k } + ϵ i , t , (5) where W i , t denotes the wealth of individual i at the end of year t ⁠ , α i and β i , t are individual and year-by-cohort fixed effects, and ϵ i , t is an error term.

I validate the research design in two steps. First, I plot the estimates ofγk⁠, the change in wealthWi,tover the event-time. Second, I estimate a restricted version of Equation (5), in which I set all lead coefficientsγk<0,∀k<0to zero. Under the identifying assumption of random event-time, the residual sums of squares from the restricted and unrestricted specifications should not be statistically different from each other. In all specifications, I fail to reject the null of parallel trends.14

Having validated the research design, I set all lead coefficients γ k < 0 , ∀ k < 0 to zero, which increases the precision of the estimated treatment effects, γ k ≥ 0 (see Borusyak and Jaravel 2017 ). Because it is unlikely that the treatment effects are equal for all k > 0 ⁠ , I estimate the regression for each k separately on the subsample of the data that includes observations with K i , t = k ⁠ , as well as the entire control group K i , t < 0 ⁠ . To accommodate heterogeneous treatment effects, I include an interaction variable I i that takes the value of one if individual i belongs to a particular group (e.g., having a parent diagnosed with a cancer with high mortality risk). The assignment of an individual to a group is either time invariant (e.g., mortality risk) or measured prior to treatment (e.g., wealth group). The resultant specification is ln ⁡ W i , t = α i + β i , t + ( γ k + η k I i ) D i , t + ϵ i , t . (6)

To give an example, consider regressing the log of children’s wealth (⁠ln⁡Wi,t⁠), measured one year after one of the parents in the treatment group was diagnosed with cancer (⁠k=1⁠), on a treatment dummy (⁠Di,t⁠) and the product of the treatment dummy and the interaction variableIi⁠. The interaction variableIitakes the value of one if the parent is diagnosed with a high mortality risk cancer. In this case, the coefficientγkin Equation (6) shows the effect of having a parent diagnosed with cancerk=1years ago on the financial wealth of children. The coefficientηkin Equation (6) shows the incremental effect of the high-risk diagnosis.

I make two small adjustments to the research design for the Dutch setting. First, the Dutch data comprise 86 different medical diagnoses. The same person can be diagnosed with multiple diseases in the same year, and in rare cases, with the same diagnosis in multiple years (approximately 4 % of the observations). In the regression, I consider each individual diagnosis-year observation as a different “pseudo-individual”, but cluster standard errors at individual level. For example, if an individual is diagnosed with breast cancer and another condition in 2013, I consider these two separate observations, but with a correlated error term. For the Dutch data i ⁠ , refers to these “pseudo-individuals”. Second, instead of an event study analysis, where each observation serves both as a treated and a control unit, I compare the outcomes of individuals diagnosed in 2013 (treated) with the outcomes of those diagnosed in 2016 (control). Adding a control group that is never treated in the period I study (which ends in 2015) gives a dynamic difference-in-differences specification, which does not suffer from any identification problems. The treatment effect, in this case, is just the difference in outcome between the two experimental groups by calendar year. The regression specification is as follows: ln ⁡ W i , t = α i + β i , t + ∑ k = 2007 2015 γ k D i + ϵ i , t , (7) where D i is again the treatment indicator. 15
article.3. Data and Variable Definitions:
3.1 Cancer and expected mortality

There is considerable variation in expected mortality across cancer diagnoses. The cancer stage and the 5-year relative survival probability are two standard measures of expected survival.

The probability of surviving cancer generally decreases in the cancer stage. The internationally accepted TNM Classification of Malignant Tumors operates using four stages. Stage 1 refers to a small cancer or a tumor that has not grown deep into nearby tissue and has not spread to the lymph nodes, or other parts of the body. Stage 2 and stage 3 indicate larger cancers or tumors that have grown more deeply into nearby tissue. These cancers may have spread to the lymph nodes, but not to other body parts. Stage 4 means that the cancer has spread to other organs, or parts of the body.

In addition to the cancer stage classification, cancer patients can form expectations about future life expectancy from publicly available estimated 5-year survival rates. Relative survival measures excess mortality, defined as the observed survival in a group of patients, divided by the survival of a comparable group in the general population (Hakulinen 1982). In Norway, such information is available atwww.Kreftlex.no.16It is a user-friendly website for survival expectations. For each main cancer type and stage, you can find information about 5-year survival rates.

To validate the use of the full spectrum of cancer diagnoses (cancer type and stage of cancer) as instruments for expected mortality of different intensity, I compare the realized deaths with the two measures of expected survival; cancer stage and 5-year survival rates.Figure 1shows that both cancer stage and 5-year survival rates are strong predictors of mortality.17

3.2 Estimating expected mortality

In Dutch data, expected mortality must be estimated. In the following, I explain the estimation procedure. I also explain how I classify diseases into two groups with different survival expectations but with the same demand for informal care.

The first step is to shrink the cross-section of diseases. I observe all medical diagnoses made by medical specialists (excluding general practitioners and mental health specialists). The diagnosis coding is hierarchical; the system recognizes 28 medical specialties (such as surgery, neurology, and cardiology) and 2,700 diagnostic codes within these specialties. Unlike the ICD-10 codes, diagnostic codes in the Dutch health care system are not consistent between medical specialties. For this reason, I use a grouping scheme provided by the Dutch Healthcare Authority (NZa) to sort the 2,700 diagnostic codes into 341 medically consistent groups, of which 307 are initially present in my sample. Among these groups, I only consider diagnostic codes with at least 500 people (from my baseline sample) diagnosed in 2013 and 2016. This step reduces the number of diagnostic codes present in the sample from 307 to 125, as rare diagnoses are omitted. Subsequently, I regress an indicator of death between 2013 and 2016 on indicators for the 125 diagnostic codes while controlling for age-gender fixed effects. This procedure gives 86 diagnostic codes with negative impact on life expectancy. Finally, each diagnosis is classified as “Low mortality” or “High mortality” using the median as the cutoff point. The average excess mortality in each group is about 2.8%and 18.2%⁠.

To test whether demand for informal care influences the relationship between increases in mortality risk and subsequent inter vivo transfers, I merge the estimated excess mortality with survey data on informal care. The data source is the 2016 Health Survey (GEMON); a nationwide survey of about 400,000 responses from people above 19 years. For those above 65 years, the survey asks about informal care. I use the answers to question “Have you received informal care in the last 12 months?” as a dependent variable. I estimate the demand for informal care following the same procedure as for mortality, described above. The construction of “Low mortality” and “High mortality” groups conditional on informal care demand is as follows. Diagnoses are first divided into high- and low-care demand groups, again with the median as the cutoff point. Then I split each group into group-specific median mortality estimates to form the “Low mortality” and “High mortality” groups. Overall, the excess mortality rate and the need for informal care estimates are consistent with folk wisdom and anecdotal evidence from the popular press.

To provide a few examples, the most prevalent diagnostic group comprises diagnoses classified as I49, I45, and I47 under the ICD-10 system (“Other cardiac arrhythmias,” “Other conduction disorders,” and “Paroxysmal tachycardia”). According to the Dutch coding system, this group includes four codes, all of which are related to cardiac arrhythmia (e.g., “Atrial fibrillation/flutter”). The estimated excess mortality of this diagnostic group is 2.6%⁠, and the estimated excess demand for informal care is 1.6%⁠. In other words, the excess death per 1,000 individuals diagnosed in 2013 with conditions that fell under this diagnostic group was 26. The excess demand for informal care was 16 (after controlling for age and gender fixed effects, and the other medical diagnoses received in 2013). The deadliest diagnosis group falls under C15–C26 (“Malignant neoplasms of digestive organs”) in the ICD-10 classification, with an estimated excess mortality of 55%⁠. The three most frequent Dutch codes for this group are 313-0904 (“Malignancy esophagus/cardia”), 313-0964 (“Pancreatic malignancy”), and 313-0979 (“Other gastrointestinal malignancies”). Examples of high demand for informal care are ICD-10 codes F03 (“Unspecified dementia”), R41 (“Other symptoms and signs involving cognitive functions and awareness”), and G30 (“Alzheimer’s disease”), with an estimated “excess demand” for informal care of 26%⁠. This group comprises three Dutch codes that all relate to “Memory problems and dementia,” including Alzheimer’s disease and vascular dementia.

3.3 Definition of wealth

Liquid assets and declared transfers by parents to their children are the primary dependent variables. Liquid assets are constructed followingCalvet and Sodini (2014)and defined as the sum of safe and risky financial assets. Safe assets are the sum of bank account balances and money market funds. Directly held stocks and risky mutual funds constitute risky financial assets. I sometimes refer to declared transfers as donations and inter vivo transfers as simply transfers. I use the terms “liquid assets” and “financial wealth” interchangeably.

For the analysis of this study, financial wealth offers several advantages compared to net worth. First, home equity, cabins, etc., can be transferred to the next generation, too; and such transfers are often organized through legal contracts invisible before the parent’s death. Second, according toKopczuk and Lupton (2007), many individuals think of their home as a future bequest and consume a greater share of their financial wealth than they would otherwise. Therefore, finding that individuals respond to mortality risk by increasing financial transfers would be a clear indication of bequest motives. Third, a considerable proportion of individuals have negative net worth in both countries, mainly reflecting measurement errors in nonliquid assets. Focusing on financial wealth, which is nonnegative, makes it possible to estimate the regressions in logs without restricting the sample. The log specification has several advantages compared to the level specification. First, it converts the skewed wealth variable into one that is approximately normal, facilitating the estimation of standard errors and reducing concerns about the disproportional impact of a few observations on the coefficient estimate. Second, for Norway, where the inference is based on the wealth paths of the children, the coefficient estimates from the log specification can be approximately linked to a change in the rank of the wealth distribution caused by treatment. Letγ^be the estimated logarithmic percentage difference in wealth between children with and without a parent diagnosed with cancer. Then, the change in the child’s rank in the wealth distribution induced by inheritance is at least−γ^+12γ^2⁠, assuming a Pareto distribution for wealth.18

article.4. Results:
The main results are presented in three sections. The first section illustrates parallel trends and presents average effects. I then estimate how inter vivo transfers respond to mortality risk across the wealth distribution. The last section sheds light on the relationship between mortality risk and inter vivo transfers.

4.2 Bequest and the moderating role of wealth

The purpose of this section is to test whether wealth influences how mortality risk affects transfers and bequests. I assign individuals to wealth groups based on their net worth before treatment followingDruedahl and Martinello (2018).22In the regressions, I need to balance having enough observation against the need to create unequal groups in terms of wealth. For that reason, I classify poor parents as those in the bottom 60th percentile, and rich parents as those in the top 20th percentile.

4.3 Bequest and the mediating role of mortality

I now provide additional tests of the predicted causal link between mortality risk and transfers. For the Norwegian sample, I restrict the analysis to parents in the top 20%of the wealth distribution. To focus on inter vivo transfers, the interaction variable,Ii⁠, includes two proxies for the severity of the diagnosis. The first proxy is based on publicly available 5-year relative survival rates (⁠RS)⁠. The second proxy is the stage of cancer. In the first case, the interaction variable takes the value of one ifRSis below 50%⁠. Individuals in this group are less than 50%likely to be alive 5 years after diagnosis. In the second case, the interaction variable takes the value of one if the parent is diagnosed with cancer at a stage higher than two, and zero otherwise. I also include a mortality indicator, labeled “Death dummy.” It takes the value of one if the diagnosed individual dies in a given year. The “Death dummy” provides a simple way of isolating inter vivo transfers; because it “controls” for the inheritance received when the parent dies.23Table 8presents the results.

It is a clear link between expected and realized mortality, and the size of the increase in children’s financial wealth. The coefficients that show the effect of expected mortality on children’s financial wealth are always positive, and mostly statistically significant. Expected mortality, instrumented by the severity of the diagnosis, lines up with changes in the wealth of children. As expected, the coefficient for the “Death dummy” is large and statistically significant.

The first three specifications show that, on average, having a parent diagnosed with a cancer with high mortality risk (i.e., 5-year relative survival rate of less than 50%⁠) increases the younger generation’s financial wealth by about 10%to 30%⁠, depending on the number of years after the diagnosis. The corresponding change in the rank of wealth distribution is approximately 10%to 25%(recall that the wealthiest individual has rank 1). The mean coefficients are, in comparison, small and statistically insignificant. Taken together, the large interaction coefficient and the small mean effect suggest that expected mortality triggers transfers, not the health crisisper se. Columns 4 to 6 report the results using the cancer stage as a proxy for high mortality risk. Although these coefficients are smaller and less significant, the interaction effects remain above the mean effect. Finally, the coefficient for the “Death dummy” variable is large, typically close to 100%⁠, which suggests that most of the inheritance is received during the year the parent dies. For an average child, the death of a parent decreases the child’s rank in the wealth distribution by 50%⁠.

4.4 Alternative mechanisms

Previous research suggests that mechanisms outside of pure altruism may explain my findings. I first investigate the role of strategic bequests and then the role of precautionary saving after health shocks.

Bernheim, Shleifer, and Summers (1985)develop a model in which parents can use bequests strategically to influence childrens’ behavior, an alternative mechanism outside pure altruism. The underlying assumption is that the parents can credibly signal to the children that they may lose their inheritance if they misbehave.Barczyk and Kredler (2018)empirically document the importance of family-provided care and its relation to expected and realized inheritance. Using data from the Health and Retirement Study, they show that declared transfers are well explained by the opportunity cost of the children to provide informal care to their parents. In other words, the main part of declared transfers takes place within families of wealthy parents who have children with low opportunity costs in the labor market.

The results inBarczyk and Kredler (2018)motivate my first robustness check. The goal is to distinguish financial gifts from payments for services by comparing the gross salaries of children in the treatment and control groups. If the observed saving behavior mainly reflects payments for services provided by the children, I expect the children to reduce labor supply; so that their gross income declines. Since I do not observe the income of children in the Netherlands, the following analysis is based only on Norway.Table 10presents the results.

The child’s gross income appears unrelated to the health status of the parent. The various subset specifications show that the hypothesis of similar gross income trajectories for children in the treatment and control group cannot be rejected, regardless of the child’s gender. For example, the coefficient estimates based on a sample of women with rich parents, a group we would expect to respond to an increase in demand for informal care, is statistically indistinguishable from those based on men with less wealthy parents. Overall, the estimates inTable 10do not provide any evidence in favor of the children altering their labor supply in response to the parent being diagnosed with cancer. In conclusion, the role of the exchange motive on inter vivo transfers is limited in this study.24

Precautionary savings and uncertain life expectancy among those diagnosed with cancer is another alternative channel that could explain why a cancer diagnosis has little impact on the wealth of the dynasty. In this case, the most likely scenario would be that cancer survivors increase their savings to self-insure against the risk of a long life with reduced earnings capacities (from working part-time in retirement). Note, however, that this mechanism would not explain inter vivo giving documented in the previous sections. Yet, to examine this possibility, I estimate the effect of cancer on total gross income, which is the sum of labor income, pension, and government transfers. I run separate regressions for the rich and the poor, and individuals with one child, and those with more than one child. After controlling for death, the main effects are small in absolute value and mostly statistically insignificant. The various subset specifications show that the hypothesis that total income in retirement is independent of health cannot be rejected, regardless of wealth and the number of children. To conclude, it seems unlikely that self-insurance motives would explain my results. All the results are in theInternet Appendix.
article.5. Quantification of the Bequest Motive:
I now estimate the model outlined in Section2.1. The aim is to provide bequest parameters that reflect the reduced-form evidence. I use indirect inference to provide a tight mapping between the reduced-form results and the structural estimation. The idea is to compare a vector of selected difference-in-differences estimates and summary statistics with a vector of similar statistics produced by the model. I focus on matching the results from the Dutch data, which are based on declared inter vivo transfers. I create a simulated counterpart to the Dutch data set, which decouples the structural estimation from the proprietary data. A short description of the simulation is in AppendixC. TheInternet Appendixcontains all details about the model and the estimation.

5.1 Identification of bequest parameters

To estimate the model presented in Section 2.1 , I need explicit functional forms of the utility functions. Following De Nardi (2004) , Cocco, Gomes, and Maenhout (2005) , and many others, I assume time-additive utility function for personal consumption: u ( C ) = C 1 − γ 1 − γ . (8)

The utility function for inter vivo transfers is the same as for bequest, which is based on Ameriks et al. (2011) , Lockwood (2018) following the analysis in Kraft, Munk, and Weiss (2021) : ν ( X ) = a 1 − σ ( X a + ϕ ) 1 − σ . (9)

Xis a financial gift net of tax in the form of inter-vivos (i.e.,X=τG(G)⁠) or bequest (i.e.,X=τB(RE+W))⁠. The parameters of interest areθ≡{ϕ,a,σ}⁠, whereϕmeasures the minimum consumption level, below which, no one intent to leave a bequest,ameasures the strength of the bequest motive, andσdetermines the risk aversion toward bequests. Bequests are luxury goods ifσ=γandϕ>0⁠, or ifσ<γ(Lockwood 2012). To limit the number of parameters to be estimated, I followAmeriks et al. (2011)and fix the risk aversion coefficientγ⁠, but perform sensitivity analysis with respect to it.

I now relax the assumption that the tax functions are differentiable in the inheritance amount. The functional forms for the tax functions are specified below. Inter vivo transfers after tax is given by τ G ( G t ) = { G t if G t ≤ G ¯ 1 G ¯ 1 + ( G t − G ¯ 1 ) ( 1 − τ G , 1 ) if G ¯ 1 < G t ≤ G ¯ 2 G ¯ 1 + ( G ¯ 2 − G ¯ 1 ) ( 1 − τ G , 1 ) + ( G t − G ¯ 2 ) ( 1 − τ G , 2 ) if G t > G ¯ 2 , (10) where G ¯ 1 , G ¯ 2 , τ G , 1 ⁠ , and τ G , 2 are parameters given in the Internet Appendix . The function τ B that maps a gross bequest into an after-tax amount is similar. To simplify notation, let B = R E + W be the total bequest, which net of tax becomes τ B ( B t ) = { B t if B t ≤ B ¯ 1 B ¯ 1 + ( B t − B ¯ 1 ) ( 1 − τ B , 1 ) if B ¯ 1 < B t ≤ B ¯ 2 B ¯ 1 + ( B ¯ 2 − B ¯ 1 ) ( 1 − τ B , 1 ) + ( B t − B ¯ 2 ) ( 1 − τ B , 2 ) if B t > B ¯ 2 , (11) where B ¯ 1 , B ¯ 2 , τ B , 1 ⁠ , and τ B , 2 are in the Internet Appendix .

The final step is to develop an identification strategy for the bequest parameters θ ≡ { ϕ , a , σ } ⁠ . Equating the first-order conditions for consumption and transfers stated in Equations ( 2 ) and ( 3 ), and using the functional forms given in Equations ( 8 ) and ( 9 ) gives τ G ( g ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) ) = a max { c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) γ σ − ϕ , 0 } (12) where c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) γ σ is “scaled” personal consumption—that is, a monotonic transformation of personal consumption ( ⁠ c ⋆ ( S t , t ; θ ) ⁠ ).

Equation (12) is informative about possible identification strategies. It shows that the parameterϕis like a strike price of a call option on a stock. Suppose that the parent’s scaled consumption, partly a function of mortality expectations and financial wealth, is belowϕ⁠. Then, the option is “out-of-the-money,” and the children do not receive an inheritance. If the parent is wealthy enough to increase scaled consumption aboveϕ⁠, in response to a change in mortality risk, then the children will also receive a financial gift.25As a result, the parameterϕis identifiable from the effect mortality has on the likelihood of doing inter vivo transfers. The size of the inheritance is determined by the strength of the bequesta⁠. Consequently, the parameterais identifiable from the level of transfers.

The option analogy and the resultant identification strategy work well when the bequest exponentσis equal to risk aversionγ⁠. Allowing the bequest exponentσto be a free parameter, which I do, complicates the identification strategy. The reason is that it can increase the unique sets of parameters that can reproduce the auxiliary parameters in the data.

5.2 The auxiliary statistics

The auxiliary statistics are based on individuals in financial wealth groups 2, 3, and 4 (i.e., 50th–98th percentile). Using the 50th–98th percentile of the financial wealth distribution speeds up the estimation without losing much information. Those below the median in the financial wealth distribution essentially do not give inter vivo transfers, and those in the top 1%often use different intergenerational transfer strategies.

With that sample, I choose the following auxiliary statistics. First, I select the estimate of the effect mortality risk has on the probability of doing inter vivo transfers about 1.5 years after the typical individual in the treatment group received negative news about personal health. These estimates correspond to those for 2014 inTable 7. For individuals below the median in the financial wealth distribution, the coefficient is close to zero, suggesting that consumption remains belowϕ⁠. The same estimate for wealth group 2 (50th–74th percentile) is 0.64, and 1.82 percentage points for wealth group 3 (75th–89th percentile). Because each financial wealth group consists of people with high and low pension income and high and low net worth, both determinants of consumption and transfers, one estimate is enough to pin downϕ⁠. I use the estimate of 1.82 percentage points for group 3 (75th–89th percentile).

As the second auxiliary statistic, I select the level counterpart to the extensive margin regression inTable 7.26I use the estimate of 908 EUR for group 2 (50th–74th percentile), which is 343 EUR per child. This parameter prevents combinations of bequest parameters that lead to big differences in transfers between individuals in good and poor health.

To identify the strength of the bequest motivea⁠, I use the first and second moments of the natural logarithm of transfers per child among the givers.27Matching the conditional mean ensures that the model delivers a realistic gift size, while targeting its dispersion prevents a completely unrealistic distribution of gifts.

5.3 The indirect inference estimator

The indirect inference estimator is defined as follows. Let γ ^ denote the 4 × 1 vector of auxiliary statistics from the real data and let γ ^ h ( θ ) denote the vector of auxiliary statistics obtained from the simulated data set h = 1 , . . . , H ⁠ . The mean across H samples is γ ^ ( θ ) ≡ H − 1 ∑ h γ ^ h ( θ ) ⁠ . The indirect inference estimator of the bequest parameters θ ≡ { ϕ , a , σ } is defined by choosing a value of θ ⋆ ≡ { ϕ ⋆ , a ⋆ , σ ⋆ } for which the moment conditions (i.e., | γ ^ − γ ^ ( θ ) | ⁠ ) are minimized: θ ⋆ ( Ω ) = arg ⁡ min θ { ( γ ^ − γ ^ ( θ ) ) ′ Ω ( γ ^ − γ ^ ( θ ) ) } . (13)

Here,Ωis a diagonal weighting matrix defined as follows: the weights equal the inverse of the squared auxiliary statistics for the regression estimates and the standard deviation of log transfers, and one for the logarithmic mean transfer. With that weighting matrixΩ⁠, the moment conditions are expressed as percentage deviations from the target parameter. Such normalization prevents some moment conditions from receiving a high weight simply because of their units.

5.4 Results

Table 11presents the results for the estimation. The first row shows that the strength of the bequest motiveaand the parameterϕare well identified with reasonable parameter values. Although caution is advisable when comparing bequest motives between studies (for a discussion, seeLockwood 2018), some conclusions stand out. With the bequest exponentσequal to the risk aversion coefficientγof five, the estimated personal consumption threshold for doing inter vivo transfer is precisely estimated with a coefficient ofϕ^=46,100EUR. The threshold equals two-thirds (one-fifth) of the mean financial wealth among individuals in the 75th to 89th (90th–98th) percentile of the financial wealth distribution, or, alternatively, comparable to the pension income in the top decile of the pension income distribution. This suggests that bequests are effectively a “luxury good,” consistent with the general conclusions of the previous analysis. The estimated strength of the bequest motive (⁠a^⁠) is 14.1, with a standard error of 1.47. The estimate ofais quite close toLockwood (2018)but lower thanAmeriks et al. (2011).28The next row reports the case in which the bequest exponentσis also estimated. The estimated bequest exponentσ^is 5.04 with a standard error of 0.05. Thus, we cannot reject that the bequest exponentσequals risk aversionγat any conventional significance level.

The central insight is that estimated bequest parameters reveal a strong bequest motive for the wealthier part of the population. To see this, consider a simplified case with no uncertainty, expenditures, and zero interest rates. Ignoring discounting, a single retiree withTremaining years to live and zero illiquid assets would give:a(1+a)(Y−ϕ+T−1Wt)(⁠Yrefers to pension income andWtrefers to financial wealth). I perform several sensitivity checks. Because there are no reasons to transfer wealth without bequest motives, it makes little sense to estimate the model under the restriction ofa=0⁠. Instead, I report the auxiliary statistics for eight misspecified models. The models are misspecified because the bequest parameters differ from the optimal ones.

In the first row, the minimum consumption threshold is onlyϕ=5,000EUR, which is far below the minimum pension. The strength of the bequest motivea=1⁠. The reason is that it is always optimal to give something, which eliminates the effect of mortality on the likelihood of doing inter vivo transfers. In other words, homothetic bequest preferences do not match the data well.

The following two rows show the results from setting the strength of the bequest motiveato its optimal value while varying the “luxury” parameterϕbetween 5,000 and 60,000 EUR. With a lowϕ⁠, the first difference-in-differences coefficientγ^1remains zero. As expected, a strong bequest motiveadoes not alter the previous conclusion. article.6. Conclusion:
I estimate the impact of negative health events on wealth dynamics and intergenerational transfers. These approximately 700,000 health shocks show a broad variation in their impact on life expectancy. Together with progressive taxation of inter vivo transfers, shocks to expected mortality are informative about bequest motives. The reason is simple. If individuals save mainly to hedge against personal lifespan uncertainty, personal wealth will be responsive to changes in expected mortality, but heirs’ wealth will be unaffected. In contrast, if individuals have bequest motives, the total wealth of the parent and the children will be less responsive to changes in the expected mortality of the parent, and heirs’ wealth is likely to increase.

Increases in expected mortality positively affect inter vivo transfers for individuals above the median in the wealth distribution but do not matter much for less wealthy individuals. A novelty with these estimates is that they reflect what individuals do, not what they say they have done in the past, or plan to do in the future. Therefore, the inference is not subject to the critique byKopczuk and Lupton (2007)regarding whether individuals can accurately distinguish their inherited wealth from wealth stemming from their labor income or concerns about systematic understatements of inter vivo transfers.

I use indirect inference to map the causal effect estimates into bequest parameters typically used in life cycle models. My estimates imply a strong bequest motive for the wealthier part of the population. Future research can improve the identification of bequest parameters by adding additional data. For example, with transaction data on consumption, one would have more power to separately identify the strength of the bequest motive, the “luxury” parameter, and the bequest exponent. Specifically, the lower the bequest exponent is relative to the exponent of the utility function, the higher the ratio of inter vivo giving to late-in-life consumption. Such variation would add considerable power to identify bequest functions and their parameters.

My estimates have implications for financial decision-making.Nakajima and Telyukova (2017)show that retirees without bequest motives are over 17 times more likely to take reverse mortgage loans. In their model, bequest motives dampen the demand for reverse mortgages because retirees want to keep their wealth to pass it on to their heirs.Heimer, Myrseth, and Schoenle (2019)find that survival expectations correlate with heterogeneity in financial education and investment behavior. They postulate that biased mortality expectations can explain the low saving rates at the beginning of the life cycle, and high saving rates among retirees. While they find that the variation in survival expectations explains the variation in saving rates, after controlling for bequest motives, the utility loss from distorted survival expectations depends on the strength of the bequest motive.Koijen, Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016)use a standard life cycle model to document major welfare costs associated with deviating from an optimal bundle of insurance products. Notably, the portfolio of insurances is crucially dependent on the strength of the bequest motive.Brandsaas (2018)estimates a life cycle model in which children and parents interact without commitment. He shows that parents place great emphasis on the well-being of their children and that these intergenerational links can affect the risk tolerance of the children.

At the macro-level, my results suggest that changes in expected mortality have a limited impact on the wealth of the dynasty. Economic models of wealth and inequality should recognize the pivotal role of intergenerational transfers and the concentration of these transfers among the upper part of the wealth distribution.

Appendix B

I show the relationship between the difference-in-differences estimator and the wealth-rank distribution. Start by characterizing the wealth distribution with a Pareto distribution. Recall that the complementary cumulative distribution function (cdf) of a Pareto distribution is P ( W e a l t h > W ) = ( W m i n W ) α ⁠ , defined on the interval [ W m i n , ∞ ) ⁠ . 30 The power law mimics the Pareto distribution by replacing the probability P ( W e a l t h > W ) with the empirical relative frequency, R a n k ( i ) N ≅ ( W m i n W ) α ⁠ . Taking logs on both sides, gives a linear relationship between the log of the empirical relative frequency and the log of normalized wealth ( ⁠ W m i n W ⁠ ). Appendix C

I create a simulated counterpart to the Dutch data set. Using simulated distributions instead of actual distributions decouples the structural estimation from the proprietary data. I start by simulating initial financial wealth (⁠W⁠) for each individual in each financial wealth group. I set the number of individuals in each group equal to the number of treated and control subjects in the data. For example, I have 95,121 individuals in financial wealth group 2 (i.e., between 50th and 74th percentile), and treated individuals make up 56.4%of my total sample. As a result,95,121×0.564treated and95,121×(1−0.564)control individuals are included in financial wealth group 2. I assume that financial wealth is lognormally distributed for a given financial wealth group. Given the distributional assumption, I match the wealth levels at the bottom 10%and the top 90%of the actual distribution.

I assume that the age distribution is normally distributed with a mean of 78 years and a standard deviation of 6, motivated by the descriptive statistics. I truncate the distribution so that all individuals are 60 years or older. Now, all individuals have financial wealth and age.

The final step is to assign pension incomeYand illiquid wealthREto each individual, which I do following a procedure outlined in theInternet Appendix. In short, individuals in the 50th–98th financial wealth percentile represent three levels of pension incomeY(in EUR thousands net of tax: 13.7, 16.9, 26.4) and three levels of illiquid wealthRE(in EUR thousands: 0, 91.9, 175). This gives nine unique groups. Within each financial wealth group, the proportion of low, medium, and high pension income corresponds to that in the actual data. Illiquid wealthREdepends on the financial wealth group and is equal to the group median.
